content:
First, label the elements of M in increasing order by a₁,a₂,a₃,… where we skip all multiples of 3.  One checks easily that

 if k=2p+1 is odd then  
 a_{k}=3p+1,  
and if k=2p is even then  
 a_{k}=3p–1.

We want
 S:=a_{k}+a_{k+1}+⋯+a_{k+2n–1}=300
for some k,n∈ℕ.  One splits into two parity‐cases for k and finds in each case a Diophantine condition of the form

Case I (k odd, k=2p+1):      3n(n+2p)=300  ⇒  n(n+2p)=100.
Case II (k even, k=2p):      3n(2p+n–1)=300  ⇒  n(2p+n–1)=100.

Since n>0 and p≥0 (resp. p≥1), one checks all divisors n of 100 and sees that the only positive n admitting an integral p are

n=1,2,4,5,10.

Hence the only solutions are

n∈{1,2,4,5,10}. 

Answer:  \boxed{1,2,4,5,10}.
